planting.note: Direct sow in fall or stratify seeds for 60 days and sow in early spring in dry, lean, sandy or rocky, acidic soil; eastern silvery aster is a specialist of well-drained, infertile, sandy or rocky soils and declines quickly in rich, moist conditions. It spreads slowly by rhizomes in appropriate dry soil.

directions: Plant in dry, lean, sandy or rocky, acidic to slightly acid soil in partial shade to full sun; eastern silvery aster is one of the most distinctive fall-blooming natives of the southeastern Coastal Plain, covered in a dense silver-gray pubescence (fine silky hairs) on the stems, leaves, and involucres that gives the entire plant a soft, felted, gray appearance even before the lavender-pink flowers open in late summer and fall. This distinctive silver foliage makes it attractive from spring through frost as a low-growing, silvery-textured specimen in dry garden conditions. It grows naturally in dry, sandy pine flatwoods and open woodlands along the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain — one of its few real habitats — making it unusually appropriate for zone 9a gardens in lean, sandy soils. The flowers attract migrating monarchs and numerous native bees. Avoid fertilizer and irrigation, which cause floppy, unattractive growth.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic soil; late purple aster self-sows moderately in appropriate conditions and thrives in dry, rocky or sandy soils in full to partial sun. It grows naturally in open woodlands, roadsides, and rocky outcrops across the eastern US.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to light shade; late purple aster is one of the most recognizable autumn wildflowers of the eastern United States, distinguished from other fall asters by its clasping leaves — each leaf wraps around the stem at its base in an ear-like fashion — and its intensely clear violet-purple ray flowers that are among the deepest, most saturated shades of any fall aster. It blooms from mid-September into November, providing critical nectar for migrating monarch butterflies and late-season native bumblebees precisely when other late-season resources are diminishing. The plant grows naturally in dry, open woods, rocky roadsides, and pine barrens across the eastern US and is well adapted to zone 9a conditions when planted in well-drained, lean soil. Combine with native grasses, goldenrods, and ironweeds for an outstanding autumn pollinator meadow.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall on well-drained to moist, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil; rice button aster self-sows prolifically once established and tolerates a wide range of soil moisture from moist meadows to moderately dry areas. It spreads by rhizomes to form colonies in appropriate conditions.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to light shade; rice button aster is one of the most floriferous and adaptable of the native fall asters, producing clouds of tiny white to pale lavender flowers with yellow centers (the disk turns reddish with age) from late August through November on much-branched, bushy stems. The individual flowers are noticeably small — about the size of a grain of rice — but they are produced in such enormous numbers that a single plant in full bloom appears to be covered in a white mist. This is one of the most important late-season nectar plants in the Southeast, attracting dozens of bee species, butterflies, and other pollinators when few other wildflowers remain in bloom. The abundant seeds feed songbirds into winter. Native across the entire eastern US from the Gulf Coast to New England, it is reliably adapted to zone 9a gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring after last frost in well-drained, dry, rocky, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Lindheimer's ironweed grows naturally on dry limestone slopes, cedar glades, and rocky calcareous soils of central Texas. It is significantly more drought-tolerant than eastern ironweeds.

directions: Plant in well-drained, dry to moderately dry, rocky or sandy, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Lindheimer's ironweed is a Texas endemic named for Ferdinand Jacob Lindheimer, the 'father of Texas botany,' and one of the most heat- and drought-tolerant ironweeds in North America. While the more common New York and tall ironweeds require moist, fertile soils, this species is adapted to the thin, rocky limestone soils and extreme drought of the Edwards Plateau and Gulf Coastal Plains of central and south Texas. The brilliant magenta-purple flower heads — intensely saturated even by ironweed standards — are produced from midsummer into fall on stems silvery with fine white hairs. An outstanding zone 9a perennial for dry limestone garden conditions where other ironweeds struggle. Irresistible to migrating monarch butterflies, pipevine swallowtails, and numerous native bees. Texas-specific alternative to the eastern ironweed in hot, dry gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial shade; flat-topped white aster grows naturally in moist open woods, shrubby wetland edges, and acidic bog margins across eastern North America. It spreads by rhizomes to form expanding colonies in appropriate moist, acidic conditions.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; flat-topped white aster is a distinctive tall native of moist, acidic habitats across eastern North America, immediately recognizable by its flat-topped, wide-spreading flower clusters (corymbs) in which dozens of white-rayed flowers are held in one broad horizontal plane rather than the branching or arching arrangement of most asters. This creates an unusually airy, table-like floral display at the top of sturdy, leafy stems in late summer and fall. The flat flower surface is an ideal landing platform for large swallowtail butterflies, bumblebees, and numerous native bee species that would struggle on the branching flower heads of other asters. Grows naturally in moist, open woodlands, wetland margins, and shrubby bog edges — preferring partial shade and consistent moisture. An excellent back-of-border or woodland-edge plant for moist sites where its architectural form provides height and late-season interest.
